does: This paper studies preference poisoning attacks against offline RLHF systems, specifically targeting Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). The authors analyze how flipping individual preference labels in the training data induces parameter-independent gradient shifts in log-linear DPO objectives. They exploit this property to reformulate the targeted poisoning problem as a structured binary sparse approximation challenge. The paper proposes two efficient attack methods: Binary-Aware Lattice Attack (BAL-A), which leverages lattice reduction techniques with binary constraints to identify minimal-flip adversarial perturbations, and Binary Matching Pursuit Attack (BMP-A), which adapts binary matching pursuit algorithms to find sparse label flips with coherence-based recovery guarantees and robustness certificates for bounded flip budgets.
connects: This work builds on recent concerns about vulnerabilities in RLHF pipelines, extending prior research on data poisoning attacks in machine learning to the specific setting of offline preference-based learning. It connects to the theoretical foundations of DPO and other log-linear preference models, while drawing on established techniques from compressed sensing and lattice-based optimization. The framing as a sparse binary approximation problem relates to classical results in signal recovery and sparse approximation theory. The paper also engages with existing work on robustness and adversarial vulnerabilities in RLHF systems, responding to the practical concern that pre-collected preference datasets may be susceptible to subtle manipulations.
enables: This research enables deeper understanding of fundamental vulnerabilities in offline RLHF systems and provides practitioners with concrete attack methods to evaluate and stress-test preference learning pipelines. The lattice-based and matching pursuit approaches offer efficient algorithms for identifying minimal adversarial perturbations, which can inform the design of defenses and dataset validation procedures. The paper's theoretical guarantees on recovery and robustness conditions open avenues for developing certified defenses or verification methods for preference datasets. Understanding these attack vectors supports development of more robust RLHF training procedures, poisoning-resistant aggregation schemes, and principled approaches to validating preference data integrity in deployed AI systems.
